summary: O'Neil argues that math research in academia is fundamentally subsidized by calculus teaching, which serves as the 'cash cow' that funds faculty positions. With MOOCs and online tools poised to replace traditional calculus instruction at scale, she predicts a dramatic reduction in math faculty positions—potentially over 50%—within a decade. She criticizes mathematicians for failing to recognize this threat or promote the value of their research, having never needed to justify their existence before. The post warns that without organized advocacy, math research could regress to 1850s levels, concentrated only at elite institutions.
key_points: Math research is essentially a byproduct of calculus teaching, with 85% of college math students taking only calculus
MOOCs and online tools will soon replace much of traditional calculus instruction, potentially outsourcing human support to overseas call centers
Mathematicians have never had to promote their work because calculus teaching subsidized it automatically
The disruption timeline suggests math departments will shrink rather than expand within 10 years
Effective advocacy for math research requires community organizing rather than dependence on billionaire donors
money_quotes: Math research is essentially funded through these teaching jobs.
It's called progress, and machines have been doing it for more than a hundred years, replacing skilled craftspeople.
Math research, which we've basically been getting for free all this time, as a byproduct of calculus, will be severely curtailed.

Mathematicians haven't been promoting their work for the simple reason that they haven't had to, because they had this cash cow called calculus which many of them aren't even aware of as a great thing.

We're going to think about it pretty hard and learn how to promote math research very soon, or else we're going back to 1850 levels of math research, where everyone knew each other and stuff was done by letter.
For my younger friends who are interested in going to grad school now, I'm not writing them letters of recommendation before having this talk.
key_insight: The entire edifice of academic math research rests on the fragile foundation of calculus instruction revenue, and technologists are about to kick that foundation out from under it.
